title: Initiating CIRP & Default vs. Dispute
content:
Session 2: Initiation of Corporate Insolvency Resolution Process [¬ß¬ß6,7,8,9 and 10]  
Swiss Ribbons v Union of India [2019 | SC | 2J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Multiple petitions challenged the constitutional validity of the IBC, 2016, primarily on the ground that the Code arbitrarily 
differentiates between FCs and OCs, violating Article 14.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Key challenges included: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FCs can initiate CIRP under s.7 without prior notice, while OCs must send a demand notice under s.8 and can 
initiate only under s.9, and their claims can be disputed. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OCs have no voting rights or membership in the CoC (s.21, 24). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 12A (90% CoC approval for withdrawal) is arbitrary. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.29A (ineligibility of promoters) is retrospective and unconstitutional. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT/NCLAT constitution violates separation of powers (Madras Bar Association line of cases). 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the classification between FCs and OCs violates Article 14? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether exclusion of OCs from CoC is unconstitutional? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether procedural differences in initiating CIRP (s.7 vs s.9) are discriminatory? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether s.12A (90% threshold for withdrawal) is arbitrary? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether s.29A (ineligibility bar) is unconstitutional? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the NCLT/NCLAT framework violates constitutional principles? 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC is economic legislation, so courts adopt a judicial deference / ‚Äúhands-off‚ÄĚ approach (RK Garg, economic policy 
deference). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Code is not a recovery statute but a beneficial resolution framework aimed at reviving the corporate debtor, 
separating the company‚Äôs interests from its promoters, ensuring creditor-in-control, preserving assets, and 
enabling speedy resolution. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Article 14 & FC‚ÄďOC classification 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court held the classification is not arbitrary because FCs and OCs are not similarly placed.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
The distinction is founded on intelligible differentia with a direct nexus to the Code‚Äôs objective ‚Üí 
resolution and revival of the CD, not debt recovery. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
FCs assess viability from the lending stage, conduct techno-economic analyses, monitor financial stress, 
and engage in restructuring; hence, are equipped to evaluate resolution plans. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
OCs merely supply goods/services and are primarily concerned with payment, not restructuring or 
long-term revival. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Financial debts are documented and verifiable, whereas operational debts involve frequent genuine 
disputes (quality, non-delivery, deficiency). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Thus, the classification is constitutionally valid. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Exclusion of OCs from CoC ‚Üí not unconstitutional because: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CoC‚Äôs role is commercial decision-making on resolution, requiring financial assessment and restructuring expertise, 
which FCs possess by design. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OCs are not without protection: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Must receive minimum liquidation value (s.30(2)(b)). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Regulation 38 prioritises OC payment and mandates equitable treatment. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLT/NCLAT can reject resolution plans unfair to OCs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, lack of voting rights does not mean lack of safeguards. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Difference in procedure: s.7 vs s.9 ‚Üí not discriminatory because: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč FC debts are verified through information utilities; CD is served notice, can file a reply, and contest at the s.7(5) 
satisfaction stage. 
27 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč OC debts have higher dispute potential, hence pre-CIRP notice under s.8 is necessary. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The distinction serves the purpose of filtering genuine defaults efficiently. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 90% threshold under s.12A ot arbitrary: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Insolvency involves collective resolution, not individual settlements. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A high threshold ensures consensus, prevents strategic withdrawals, and is policy driven. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT/NCLAT can judicially review unreasonable rejection of settlements. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Constitutionality of s.29A ‚Üí upheld because: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No vested right exists to be a resolution applicant. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The bar prevents defaulting promoters from regaining control and defeating insolvency objectives. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The provision is not impermissibly retrospective as it governs eligibility, not punishment. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT/NCLAT validity upheld with caution: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Following Madras Bar Association rulings, tribunal structure is constitutionally valid, but the Union must comply 
in letter and spirit with principles on appointments and independence. 
 
Conclusion 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Classification between FCs and OCs does not violate Article 14 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Exclusion of OCs from CoC is constitutionally valid 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Procedural difference under s.7 vs s.9 is justified 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.12A (90%) is valid 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.29A is valid, not retrospective, no vested right 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT/NCLAT upheld, government directed to ensure independence. 
 
Vidharbha Industries v Axis Bank [2022 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD was owed ~‚āĻ1730 crores from MERC (Maharashtra Electricity Regulator) pursuant to an appellate award (APTEL). 
The matter was pending final enforcement, but entitlement stood recognised. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FC filed a Section 7 IBC application for CIRP for default of ‚āĻ553 crores. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD argued: If the MERC money is realised, it can repay FC fully; initiating CIRP is unnecessary and premature. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLT/NCLAT admitted the Section 7 application, holding that the MERC dispute was irrelevant ‚Üí appeal to 
SC. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Under Section 7(5)(a) IBC, is admission of a CIRP application by an FC mandatory or discretionary once default is proved? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can the financial health, viability, and extraneous recoveries (like MERC dues) be considered by the AA while deciding 
admission under s.7? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 7(5)(a), IBC ‚Üí ‚Äúmay admit‚ÄĚ (FC applications) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9(5)(a), IBC ‚Üí ‚Äúshall admit‚ÄĚ (OC applications) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC scheme distinguishing FCs and OCs (based on role, risk, debt nature, impact) 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court emphasised that the word ‚Äúmay‚ÄĚ in Section 7(5)(a) creates discretion, unlike Section 9(5)(a) where ‚Äúshall‚ÄĚ makes 
admission mandatory.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč The difference is deliberate, clear, and requires no purposive re-interpretation ‚Üí literal interpretation applies. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč This distinction flows from the fundamentally different nature of FCs and OCs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč FCs invest large, long-term amounts, often secured, with interest in the enterprise‚Äôs survival.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč They can restructure, renegotiate, and absorb delays.  
28 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Because their credit risk is tied to the company‚Äôs continuing operations, the AA can examine viability factors 
[criticism: viability of the enterprise is a matter of business, and that matters of business can only be 
negotiated between creditors and debtor] before triggering insolvency.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
In this case, the pending recoverable amount (‚āĻ1730 crores) massively exceeded the default (‚āĻ553 crores), 
indicating real repayment capacity ‚Üí making it a relevant consideration, not an extraneous one. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč OCs extend short-term, unsecured credit for goods or services.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Their businesses rely on immediate turnover, and non-payment causes direct liquidity stress.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Unlike FCs, OCs also have no representation in the CoC, so mandatory admission on default is the only statutory 
safeguard that preserves their bargaining power ‚Üí this justifies the stricter rule under Section 9(5)(a). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court held that the MERC award was not a speculative or unrelated claim ‚Üí it was a judicially recognised receivable 
directly linked to repayment capacity ‚áí NCLT was therefore wrong in treating it as irrelevant. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč While admission on default is normally the standard outcome, it is not automatic for Section 7.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Discretion can be exercised in exceptional cases [but not to be used arbitrarily]‚Üí either by keeping admission in 
abeyance or declining it with liberty to refile if payment is still not made. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč On precedent: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Innoventive dealt with a statutory moratorium under state law and repugnancy issues, not discretion under Section 
7. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Swiss Ribbons upheld the constitutional validity of the FC-OC distinction, but did not rule on whether Section 7 is 
mandatory or discretionary.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
Neither judgment bars the present interpretation. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 7(5)(a) is discretionary, not mandatory. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9(5)(a) remains mandatory for OCs. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Financial health, viability, and incoming awards can be considered under Section 7 at the admission stage. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč MERC dues were relevant, and the NCLT erred by ignoring them. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The matter was remanded to NCLT for fresh consideration. 
 
Note: this judgment has been diluted in M. Suresh Kumar Reddy v. Canara Bank (2023) 
29 
 
 
No need to examine the viability ‚Üí if there‚Äôs a debt and default, must admit the application ‚Üí Innovative holds good. 
 
EPC Constructions India v Matix Fertilizers 2025 INSC 1259  
 
Preference shares do not have a debt element ‚Äď redemptions only arise out of profits or proceeds from further issues ‚Äď once EPCC 
voluntarily converted its receivables into shares, its earlier debt claim stood extinguished. The transaction was consciously restricted. 
 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč EPC Constructions (A) and Matix (R) entered into engineering and construction contracts for setting up a fertiliser 
complex. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A claimed ‚āĻ572.72 crore was due from R under on-shore and off-shore supply contracts. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Parties discussed restructuring: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
R proposed converting ‚āĻ400 crore of receivables into non-cumulative redeemable preference shares 
(NCRPS/CRPS). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A‚Äôs Board approved conversion of part of receivables into CRPS. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CIRP was initiated against A; the interim RP (later liquidator) issued a demand notice to R for ‚āĻ632.71 crore: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ‚āĻ310 crore on account of CRPS redemption 
‚ěĘ‚Äč ‚āĻ322.71 crore on account of contractual receivables 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč R disputed the entire claim, including liability on CRPS. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Liquidator obtained permission under s.33(5) to initiate proceedings and filed a Section 7 IBC application against R. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT + NCLAT dismissed the application, holding that a preference shareholder is not a financial creditor. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether amounts represented by non-cumulative redeemable preference shares (CRPS) constitute ‚Äúfinancial debt‚ÄĚ under 
Section 5(8) of the IBC? 
30 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether failure to redeem CRPS after expiry of the redemption period amounts to ‚Äúdefault‚ÄĚ under Section 3(12) of the 
IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Whether the holder of CRPS (A) qualifies as a ‚Äúfinancial creditor‚ÄĚ for the purpose of filing a Section 7 application? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Companies Act, 2013 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Preference shares = share capital, not debt. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.55: Preference shares can be redeemed only: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
out of profits available for dividend, or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
out of proceeds of a fresh issue of shares made for redemption. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč IBC 
‚ěĘ‚Äč s.5(8) ‚ÄĒ ‚ÄúFinancial debt‚ÄĚ: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Must involve disbursal against consideration for time value of money. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Includes instruments like bonds, notes, debentures ‚ÄĒ preference shares not mentioned. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.3(12) ‚ÄĒ ‚ÄúDefault‚ÄĚ: non-payment of debt which is due and payable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.7 ‚ÄĒ Application by a financial creditor. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Are CRPS a ‚Äúdebt‚ÄĚ? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Preference share capital is not a loan ‚Üí return on preference shares is dividend, payable only when the company 
makes profits. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A debt-holder receives interest irrespective of profits. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, preference shares do not possess the essential characteristics of debt. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
s.5(8)(c) mentions several debt instruments; preference shares are omitted ‚ÄĒ intentionally and 
significantly. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
As paid-up share capital, the amount lacks consideration for the time value of money ‚Üí fails s.5(8). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Was redemption ‚Äúdue and payable‚ÄĚ? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under s.55 CA2013, CRPS can be redeemed only: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
out of profits, or 
‚Ė†‚Äč
fresh issue proceeds. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč B had no profits and no reserves, nor a fresh issue. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even though the redemption period expired, the amount did not become ‚Äúdue and payable‚ÄĚ under law. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Hence, no ‚Äúdefault‚ÄĚ under s.3(12) IBC. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can a preference shareholder file s.7? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since preference share capital is not debt, the preference shareholder is: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
not a creditor, let alone a financial creditor. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Therefore, the s.7 petition is not maintainable. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Real nature of the transaction 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Court applies the substance-over-form test: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The conversion of receivables into CRPS changes the legal character of the claim. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A voluntarily became a preference shareholder, not a lender. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The CRPS do not constitute ‚Äúfinancial debt.‚ÄĚ 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč No default occurred, because redemption was not legally due. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The appellant is merely a preference shareholder, not a financial creditor. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 7 application is not maintainable. 
 
31 
 
Capital Limited v Sach Marketing [2024 | SC | 2J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD, a beer manufacturer, appointed SM as a Sales Promoter via an agreement dated 1 April 2014 for 12 months, creating a 
marketing-distribution relationship. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A key term required SM to deposit a security deposit of ‚āĻ53,15,000, carrying 21% interest per annum.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Another was a monthly payment of 4K from the CD to SM. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CD paid interest for multiple years, deducted TDS, and recorded the amount in its books as long-term 
loans/long-term liabilities. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CIRP commenced against the CD + the IRP partly admitted the claim as operational and partly financial.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč SM moved NCLT under Section 60(5) seeking classification entirely as a financial creditor ‚Üí rejected.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
NCLAT reversed, classifying SM as a financial creditor.  
‚Ė†‚Äč
CD appealed to the Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does the security deposit qualify as ‚Äúdebt‚ÄĚ under Section 5(11), IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč What is the test for financial debt under Section 5(8), IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč When does a debt qualify as operational debt under Section 5(21), IBC? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5(11) ‚Üí Debt = liability/obligation in respect of a claim (includes financial & operational debt). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5(7) ‚Üí Financial creditor = person to whom financial debt is owed. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5(8) ‚Üí Financial debt = debt disbursed against consideration for time value of money + categories (a‚Äďi). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Clause 5(8)(f) ‚Üí Includes transactions having commercial effect of borrowing. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5(21) ‚Üí Operational debt = claim in respect of provision of goods or services. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The security deposit is ‚Äúdebt‚ÄĚ under Section 5(11) 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The deposit was refundable with interest ‚Üí since SM had a right to payment, it qualified as a ‚Äúclaim‚ÄĚ under 
Section 3(6), making the liability a debt under Section 5(11). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Test for financial debt = (i) disbursal + (ii) time value of money 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court reiterated Anuj Jain & Pioneer Urban principles: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Disbursal must be for the use of the corporate debtor 
‚Ė†‚Äč
There must be consideration for time value of money (interest is strong evidence, though not mandatory) 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even for Section 5(8)(a‚Äďi), the core time-value-of-money test must be satisfied 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Here: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The amount was disbursed to the CD 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The deposit carried 21% interest, and TDS was deducted by CD 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The CD booked it as long-term loan/long-term liability 
‚Üí This clearly shows the time value of money. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Transaction had commercial effect of borrowing under Section 5(8)(f) 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The Court applied a substance-over-form test: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The label ‚Äúsecurity deposit‚ÄĚ is not determinative. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The parties‚Äô conduct, accounting treatment, and financial reality matter. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The deposit behaved like financing, not like a performance security. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD repeatedly raised similar deposits from private entities ‚ÄĒ indicating a financing practice. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Hence, it qualifies under 5(8)(f) as a transaction with the commercial effect of borrowing. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč It is not operational debt 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Operational debt must have a direct nexus with services provided. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Here: 
32 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The only service-linked payment was ‚āĻ4,000/month, not the security deposit ‚Üí this qualifies as OD. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The deposit had no correlation with actual service performance or value ‚Üí not an OD, rather an FD. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Overall principle applied 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A creditor is a financial creditor if: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The CD owes them a financial debt, 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The debt has time value of money, and 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The transaction functions economically like borrowing, even if structured differently. 
 
Conclusion 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The security deposit constitutes debt under Section 5(11). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč It satisfies the test of financial debt under Section 5(8) (disbursal + time value + commercial effect of borrowing). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč It is not operational debt because it has no nexus with the service rendered. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč R1 is a financial creditor, and the NCLAT decision is upheld. 
 


Session 3: Concept of ‚Äėdefault‚Äô and ‚Äėexistence of a dispute‚Äô  
Mobilox Innovations Private Limited v Kirusa Software Private Limited [2017 | SC | 2J] 
Facts:  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Mobilox was hired by Star TV to conduct Nach Baliye tele-voting ‚Äď Kirusa provided toll-free numbers; Mobilox had to pay 
rentals via Kirusa to telecom operators. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Debt ~ ‚āĻ20 lakhs owed by Mobilox. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 30 Jan 2015: Mobilox withheld payment, alleging NDA breach ‚Üí Kirusa publicly displayed association with Nach Baliye on 
its website. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Kirusa filed a S.9 IBC application to initiate CIRP. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT: Dismissed ‚ÄĒ held that the existence of a notice of dispute bars admission, tribunal cannot examine its genuineness. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT: Reversed ‚ÄĒ held dispute must be ‚Äúbona fide‚ÄĚ; found Mobilox‚Äôs defence not bona fide, remanded. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NOTE: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč All events preceded IBC enforcement (Aug 2016) ‚ÄĒ Court applies IBC without discussing retrospectivity. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč S.9(3)(c) certificate (from financial institution) was not filed initially.  
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Appeal to SC by Mobilox. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Does an alleged NDA breach + withholding payment qualify as a ‚Äúdispute‚ÄĚ under s.8‚Äďs.9 IBC? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč What is the threshold test for ‚Äúexistence of dispute‚ÄĚ? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Must the dispute be pending in suit/arbitration? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can NCLT examine merits or bona fides of the dispute? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Is the s.9(3)(c) certificate mandatory? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.5(6), IBC ‚Äď ‚ÄúDispute‚ÄĚ includes disputes on: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč existence/amount of debt, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč quality of goods/services, 
‚ěĘ‚Äč breach of representation or warranty. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.8(2)(a), IBC ‚Äď CD may notify: existence of dispute OR pendency of suit/arbitration before demand notice. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.9(5)(ii)(d), IBC ‚Äď Application must be rejected if notice of dispute is received. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.9(3)(c), IBC ‚Äď Certificate from a financial institution confirming non-payment needed only if available. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Nature of ‚Äúdispute‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The word ‚Äúincludes‚ÄĚ in s.5(6) makes the definition inclusive, not exhaustive. 
33 
 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Earlier BLRC Draft which used ‚Äúmeans‚ÄĚ and required bona fide suit/arbitration ‚Üí NOW REMOVED. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Bona fide requirement was consciously removed ‚Üí IBC does not import the Madhusudan Gordhandas 
winding-up standard. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Suit/arbitration not necessary 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The phrase in s.8(2)(a) must be read disjunctively (OR, not AND) ‚Üí requiring prior suit/arbitration would cause 
hardship; a dispute can exist without formal proceedings. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Pre-existing requirement satisfied ‚Üí Mobilox raised an NDA breach on 30 Jan 2015, before any s.8 demand notice ‚áí so 
dispute was pre-existing, not created as an afterthought. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Pre existence of dispute extends till the initiation of CIRP process. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Threshold test for ‚Äúexistence of dispute‚ÄĚ 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SC holds the adjudicating authority only checks: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Is there a plausible contention requiring investigation? 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Does the dispute truly exist in fact, and is not: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
spurious, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
hypothetical, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
illusory, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
mere bluster, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
frivolous or vexatious? 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Is the defence not a patently feeble legal argument or unsubstantiated assertion? 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Standard is greater than a mere assertion, but lower than proof on balance of probabilities (‚Äėbetween proof and 
assertion‚Äô). 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The authority must not examine merits, probability of success, or adjudicate the claim. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Application to facts 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NDA breach is a breach of representation, squarely within s.5(6). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A documentary basis existed (public webpage disclosure), so the defence was not fictitious or moonshine. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Requires investigation, therefore meets the low-threshold test. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč NCLAT erred by testing bona fides and merits, which is impermissible. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Since a real dispute existed, CIRP must be rejected at the threshold. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč s.9(3)(c) certificate 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The phrase ‚Äúif available‚ÄĚ makes it directory, not mandatory. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Objection was not raised before NCLT ‚Üí cannot be used to dismiss at appellate stage. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The AA when examining an application u/9 will have to determine: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Whether there is an ‚Äúoperational debt‚ÄĚ as defined exceeding Rs.1 lakh? (See Section 4 of the Act) [now, 1 Cr, post 
2020] 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Whether the documentary evidence furnished with the application shows that the aforesaid debt is due and payable 
and has not yet been paid? and 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Whether there is existence of a dispute between the parties or the record of the pendency of a suit or arbitration 
proceeding filed before the receipt of the demand notice of the unpaid operational debt in relation to such dispute? 
 
Macquarie Bank Limited v Shilpi Cable Technologies Limited [2017 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Macquarie Bank (‚ÄúMB‚ÄĚ), Singapore, acquired the supplier‚Äôs rights under a supply contract (assigned from Hamera 
International) against Shilpi Cable Technologies (CD). 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Goods worth USD 6.3 million were supplied.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč Two invoices were raised; payment remained outstanding. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč MB issued statutory notice under Sections 433/434 of Companies Act, 1956.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD denied liability.  
‚ěĘ‚Äč After IBC came into force, MB issued a Section 8 demand notice through a lawyer, demanding payment. CD again 
disputed liability. 
34 
 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč MB filed Section 9 IBC application. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT rejected it because: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč No Section 9(3)(c) certificate from a financial institution confirming non-payment was submitted. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč There was a pre-existing dispute. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 8 demand notice was issued by a lawyer, not the operational creditor. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLAT upheld NCLT. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč MB appealed to the Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Is Section 9(3)(c) (financial institution certificate) mandatory or directory for filing Section 9 IBC applications? 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Can a Section 8 demand notice be issued by a lawyer/authorised agent of the operational creditor? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 8, IBC ‚Üí OC may deliver demand notice on default. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9(3)(c), IBC ‚Üí Application shall include a certificate from a financial institution confirming no payment (if 
available). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Advocates Act, Section 30 ‚Üí Advocates have the right to practice, including acts connected to legal proceedings. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9(3)(c) is directory, not mandatory 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court relied on the word ‚Äúconfirming‚ÄĚ ‚Üí it indicates the certificate is evidentiary, not constitutive. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Section 9(3)(c) is not a pre-condition to triggering CIRP; it is one mode of proof of default, not the only 
mode. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Rules (Form 5, Rule 6, Annexure III) expressly say certificate is required only ‚Äúif available‚ÄĚ, showing 
legislative intent is not to make it compulsory. 
‚óŹ‚Äč
The Rules, as contemporanea expositio, clarify that the provision is procedural and supportive, 
not threshold-blocking. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
A mandatory reading would create absurd, discriminatory results, especially where: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
OCs are foreign entities, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
Their banks are not ‚Äúfinancial institutions‚ÄĚ under Section 3(14), 
‚óŹ‚Äč
And compliance is impossible despite a genuine debt. 
‚Üí Law cannot be interpreted to inconvenience innocent parties or defeat the IBC‚Äôs purpose. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
The mere use of ‚Äúshall‚ÄĚ does not automatically make a provision mandatory when: 
‚óŹ‚Äč
compliance may be impossible, 
‚óŹ‚Äč
or when the object of the statute would be defeated. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí Section 9(3)(c) is directory, not mandatory. Default can be proved through other material (emails, invoices, 
reminders etc.). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A Section 8 demand notice can be issued by a lawyer/authorised agent 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Section 8 uses the word ‚Äúdeliver‚ÄĚ, not ‚Äúissue‚ÄĚ ‚Üí broader in scope, allows delivery through an authorised 
representative. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Form 3 & Form 5 require signatures by a person ‚Äúauthorised to act on behalf of the OC‚ÄĚ, and that person can hold 
a position ‚Äúwith or in relation to‚ÄĚ the OC ‚ÄĒ a very wide phrase that includes lawyers and agents. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The Court harmoniously read IBC with Section 30 of the Advocates Act, which gives lawyers the right to practice 
and perform all actions leading to legal proceedings. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč There is no conflict between IBC and Advocates Act ‚Üí both can operate together. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč SO ‚Üí a lawyer or authorised agent can validly issue a Section 8 demand notice on behalf of an operational creditor. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The documents mandatory 
‚ěĘ‚Äč A ‚Üí mandatory 
‚ěĘ‚Äč B ‚Üí if there is notice given by CD then no 
35 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč C ‚Üí not mandatory  
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9(3)(c) is directory, not a mandatory threshold requirement. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 8 demand notices can be issued by lawyers/authorised agents. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT and NCLAT judgments set aside. Matter remanded. 
 
Rajratan Agarwal v Solartex India Private Limited [2022 | SC | 2J] 
Facts: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč OC contracted to supply ~500 MT coal to the CD for use in the FBC boiler. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Only 412 MT was supplied ‚Üí the CD alleged that the coal was of poor quality, causing boiler malfunction. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč 30.10.2016: CD (via its sister concern) emailed OC raising quality issues + attached photographs. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Multiple email exchanges followed; OC denied defects. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD stopped using further supply. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD later claimed ‚āĻ4.44 crore damages due to poor coal quality ‚Äď OC issued Section 8 IBC demand notice (03.02.2018) 
claiming ‚āĻ21.57 lakh + interest. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD replied disputing the claim and citing quality issues. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Two civil suits were also filed. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Despite this, OC filed a Section 9 application. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT admitted it; NCLAT upheld admission. CIRP was initiated. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč CD appealed to the Supreme Court. 
 
Issues: Whether a pre-existing dispute existed so as to require rejection of the Section 9 application? 
 
Rule: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 8, IBC ‚Äď CD may bring the existence of dispute to OC‚Äôs notice. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9, IBC ‚Äď IRP application must be rejected if dispute pre-exists the Section 8 notice. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 5(6), IBC ‚Äď Defines ‚Äúdispute‚ÄĚ. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Legal standard from Mobilox ‚Äď only a plausible, not spurious or patently feeble dispute is needed; merits not to be 
examined. 
 
Application: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Standard of review is very narrow ‚Üí the Court reaffirmed Mobilox: 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The AA does not decide who will win. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The AA does not test on preponderance of probability. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč The AA only checks whether the dispute is: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Plausible + not sham, spurious, hypothetical, or patently feeble. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Disputes cannot be evaluated on civil-trial standards 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Unlike civil suits, the IBC threshold is much lower. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Even weak evidence is acceptable if it shows the dispute is real, not fake. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Purpose of low standard: 
‚Ė†‚Äč
IBC is not a debt recovery mechanism. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
CIRP must not be triggered when a real commercial dispute exists. 
‚Ė†‚Äč
Low threshold ensures parties go to civil courts/arbitration, not insolvency forum, when issues are 
genuinely contested. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč SC found documentary basis for dispute existed 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Emails before Section 8 notice raised quality concerns. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Photographs were attached. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Lab reports (even from CD‚Äôs own labs) supported its claim. 
36 
 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Supply was actually stopped after 412 MT. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč CD had a consistent stand that coal was defective. 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Civil suits demanding damages were already filed. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Warranty v. condition 
‚ěĘ‚Äč Under SoGA, a stipulation in regards to goods can be a condition or a warranty, whereby condition > warranty ‚Üí 
a condition is treated as essential to the main purpose of the contract, and a breach of it gives rise to the right to 
repudiate the contract; a warranty is a stipulation in a contract collateral to its main purpose and when it is 
breached, the buyer cannot reject the goods or repudiate the contract (can only sue for damages u/s 12(3) of 
SoGA). 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Hence, the dispute was not an afterthought. 
 
Conclusion: 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč A pre-existing dispute did exist before the Section 8 notice. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč The dispute was plausible, not feeble or sham. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč Section 9 application should have been rejected. 
‚ĚĖ‚Äč NCLT & NCLAT orders set aside.
cases: Swiss Ribbons v Union of India

‚Äč s.29A (ineligibility of promoters) is retrospective and unconstitutional.

‚Äč Whether exclusion of OCs from CoC is unconstitutional?
‚Äč Whether procedural differences in initiating CIRP (s.7 vs s.9) are discriminatory?
‚Äč Whether s.12A (90% threshold for withdrawal) is arbitrary?
‚Äč Whether s.29A (ineligibility bar) is unconstitutional?
‚Äč Whether the NCLT/NCLAT framework violates constitutional principles?
‚Äč IBC is economic legislation, so courts adopt a judicial deference / ‚Äúhands-off‚ÄĚ approach (RK Garg, economic policy
‚Äč The Code is not a recovery statute but a beneficial resolution framework aimed at reviving the corporate debtor,
‚Äč Article 14 & FC‚ÄďOC classification
‚Äč The Court held the classification is not arbitrary because FCs and OCs are not similarly placed.
‚Äč Thus, the classification is constitutionally valid.
‚Äč Exclusion of OCs from CoC ‚Üí not unconstitutional because:
‚Äč CoC‚Äôs role is commercial decision-making on resolution, requiring financial assessment and restructuring expertise,
